sources.name: Strunk & White, Elements of Style — Rule 10: Use the Active Voice
sources.note: Public domain. Rule 10: active voice is more direct and vigorous than passive. Passive permitted when it determines which word becomes the subject.

sources.name: Purdue OWL — Passive Verbs
sources.note: Passive permitted when actor is unknown, irrelevant, or when topic being foregrounded is not the actor.

_description: Active voice is the default for all output. Passive voice is permitted only under specific conditions defined here. No two passive constructions may appear in sequence.

rules.rule: Use active voice as the default for all sentences.
rules.rationale: Active voice is more direct, more concise, and keeps the actor visible. It is the primary driver of readable, plain-language output.
rules.example_correct: The team closed the checkpoint at midnight.
rules.example_incorrect: The checkpoint was closed at midnight by the team.

rules.rule: Passive voice is permitted when the actor is unknown.
rules.rationale: If who performed the action cannot be determined, passive is the correct construction.
rules.example_correct: Three vehicles were stolen from the lot overnight.
rules.example_incorrect: Someone stole three vehicles from the lot overnight.
rules.note: Use passive here because the actor is genuinely unknown — not as evasion.

rules.rule: Passive voice is permitted when the actor is irrelevant to the meaning.
rules.rationale: When what happened matters more than who caused it, passive is correct.
rules.example_correct: The road was closed for repairs.
rules.example_incorrect: The city department closed the road for repairs.
rules.note: Use passive only when the actor adds no useful information to the reader.

rules.rule: Passive voice is permitted when the actor is deliberately withheld.
rules.rationale: Legal, diplomatic, or sensitive contexts may require the actor to be omitted intentionally.
rules.example_correct: Mistakes were made during the review process.
rules.note: This is a domain-specific exception. Use only when withholding the actor is a deliberate and justified decision — not a way to avoid accountability in general writing.

rules.rule: No two passive constructions may appear in sequence.
rules.rationale: Consecutive passive sentences remove all actors from the output and make prose impossible to follow. Each passive must be followed by an active construction.
rules.example_correct: The report was submitted on Thursday. The committee reviewed it the following morning.
rules.example_incorrect: The report was submitted on Thursday. It was reviewed the following morning. A decision was reached by Friday.
rules.source: Strunk & White Rule 10 — avoid making one passive depend directly upon another

rules.rule: Do not use passive to avoid naming the actor when the actor is known and relevant.
rules.rationale: Evasive passive is a clarity violation. If the actor is known and their action matters, name them.
rules.example_correct: The contractor failed to file the permits on time.
rules.example_incorrect: The permits were not filed on time.

rules.rule: Do not use there is or there are as a sentence opener when an active subject exists.
rules.rationale: There is and there are delay the actor and weaken the verb. Replace with a direct active construction.
rules.example_correct: A risk exists at every checkpoint.
rules.example_incorrect: There is a risk at every checkpoint.
rules.source: Strunk & White Rule 10 — replace there is / could be heard with active verb

rules.rule: Do not use it is or it was as a sentence opener when a direct subject exists.
rules.rationale: It is and it was constructions delay meaning and inflate sentence length.
rules.example_incorrect: It took three weeks for the decision to be made.
rules.source: Strunk & White Rule 10 and Rule 13 — omit needless words

permitted: Actor is irrelevant to the reader
Actor is deliberately withheld for legal or sensitive reasons
not_permitted: Passive used to avoid responsibility or accountability
